coredata.dc:title: Growth of CuO nanoneedles on graphene quantum dots as peroxidase mimics for sensitive colorimetric detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Li
Hai, Xin
Xia, Chang
Chen, Xu-Wei
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots-copper oxide nanocomposites (GQDs/CuO) are facilely fabricated by the growth of CuO nanoneedles on graphene quantum dots under mild conditions. The as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites could be used as an effective peroxidase mimics to catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 to produce a blue-colored solution. Investigations on the mechanism indicate that the nature of peroxidase-like activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites originates from OH radical generation. The increase of absorbance at 652nm induced by the catalytic effect of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites offers accurate detection of H2O2 in the range of 0.5–10μM, along with a detection limit of 0.17μM. A colorimetric method for serum glucose detection is also proposed by combining the GQDs/CuO catalytic reaction and the enzymatic oxidation of glucose with glucose oxidase. This method is simple, sensitive and selective for glucose detection with a linear range from 2 to 100μM with a detection limit of 0.59μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs/CuO nanocomposites
Enzyme mimics
H2O2
Glucose
Colorimetric detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth of CuO nanoneedles on GQDs
Applications of GQDs/CuO composites in H2O2 detection and glucose detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of GQDs/CuO composites.
Mechanism for the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites-H2O2-TMB system.

TEM images of GQDs (A) and GQDs/CuO nanocomposites (B). HRTEM(C), FT-IR spectra (D), XRD pattern (E) and Wide-scan XPS spectrum of GQDs and GQDs/CuO nanocomposites (F). High-resolution scan of Cu 2p (G). Raman spectra of GQDs and GQDs/CuO nanocomposites (H).

Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652nm in different reaction systems (A): TMB+GQDs/CuO nanocomposites (a), TMB+H2O2 (b), GO/CuO nanocomposites+TMB+H2O2 (c), GQDs/CuO nanocomposites+TMB+H2O2 (d). Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652nm catalyzed with the as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites, GQDs, CuO NPs, the mixture of GQDs and CuO NPs (B). Reaction conditions: 0.8mM TMB, 70μgmL−1 catalyst, 10mM H2O2 in 40mM B-R buffer (pH 3.5).

Steady-state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. The velocity of the reaction was measured using 70μgmL−1 GQDs/CuO nanocomposite in 40mM B-R buffer (pH 3.5). The error bars represent the standard error derived from three replicate measurements. The concentration of TMB was 0.8mM and the H2O2 concentration was varied (A). The concentration of H2O2 was 50mM and the TMB concentration was varied (B). Double reciprocal plots of activity of GQDs/CuO composite with the concentration of one substrate (H2O2 or TMB) fixed and the other varied (C and D).

Effect of the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites on the formation of hydroxyl radical with terephthalic acid as fluorescence probe. (a) GQDs/CuO composites(10μgmL−1) without H2O2; (b–e) GQDs/CuO composites (0, 10, 30 and 50μgmL−1) with 50mM H2O2. Reaction conditions: 0.625mM terephthalic acid and different solutions were incubated in 40mM B-R buffer (pH 3.5) at 40°C for 8h.

Dependence of the peroxidase-like activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposite on temperature (A), pH (B), TMB concentration (C). Experiments were carried out using 70μgmL−1 GQDs/CuO nanocomposites in 324μL of 40mM B-R buffer (pH 3.5) with 0.8mM TMB as substrate at 40°C. The H2O2 concentration was 50mM unless otherwise stated.

The dose-response curve for H2O2 detection under optimal conditions. Inset: the corresponding linear calibration plots for H2O2 detection (A). The linear calibration plots for glucose detection using GQDs/CuO nanocomposites as an artificial enzyme (B). Inset: images of production of colored products for different concentrations of glucose. The error bars represent the standard deviation of triplicate measurements.

Selectivity of glucose detection based on the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites (from left to right: 2.0mM mannose, 2.0mM lactose, 2.0mM fructose and 2.0mM maltose, 0.2mM glucose).
Comparison of the kinetic parameters of oxidation reaction catalyzed by reported enzyme-like nanomaterials and horseradish peroxidase (HRP).
Comparison between the current method and other reported techniques for the detection of glucose.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Color: Colorimetric method; E-chem: electrochemical method; Fluor: fluorimetric method; LOD: limit of detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Comparison of the glucose contents in serum samples by the currently colorimetric method and provided by the hospital
( n = 3 ,
m e a n s ± t ⋅ s /
n
,
f = 95 % ,
t = 4.303 )
.

head.ce:title: Growth of CuO nanoneedles on graphene quantum dots as peroxidase mimics for sensitive colorimetric detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots-copper oxide nanocomposites (GQDs/CuO) are facilely fabricated by the growth of CuO nanoneedles on graphene quantum dots under mild conditions. The as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites could be used as an effective peroxidase mimics to catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 to produce a blue-colored solution. Investigations on the mechanism indicate that the nature of peroxidase-like activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites originates from OH radical generation. The increase of absorbance at 652nm induced by the catalytic effect of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites offers accurate detection of H2O2 in the range of 0.5–10μM, along with a detection limit of 0.17μM. A colorimetric method for serum glucose detection is also proposed by combining the GQDs/CuO catalytic reaction and the enzymatic oxidation of glucose with glucose oxidase. This method is simple, sensitive and selective for glucose detection with a linear range from 2 to 100μM with a detection limit of 0.59μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes are generally proteins which are extremely efficient at catalyzing a variety of reactions with high substrate specificity, activities, and yields under mild reaction conditions. However, the utilization of natural peroxidases on a large scale remains challenging, mainly because of their harsh preparation and storage conditions, tedious purification process, high cost, and easy degradation [1,2]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to create potential alternatives such as natural enzyme mimics and artificial enzymes that not only mimic the complexity and function of natural systems but also overcome these drawbacks of natural enzymes. As a promising candidate for artificial enzymes, catalytically active nanomaterials (nanozymes) show several advantages over natural enzymes, such as low-price, easy preparation, high stability against denaturing, as well as tunability in catalytic activities [3]. Furthermore, the recent advance of nanotechnology and biotechnology opens new opportunities for the development of nanoparticulate artificial enzymes with high catalytic activity [4].

A variety of nanoscale materials, such as ferromagnetic nanoparticles [5], Co3O4 nanoparticles [6], V2O5 nanoparticles [7], gold nanoparticles [8], platinum nanoparticles [9], CoFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles [10], and CuO nanoparticles (CuO NPs) [11], have been found to possess unique enzyme-mimic catalytic activities, which have been widely used in various fields such as H2O2 detection [12], immunoassays [5], glucose detection [13], nucleic acids detection [14], and cytotoxicity assays [15]. In recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials, such as fullerene [16], carbon nanotubes [17] and graphene oxide (GO) [18] are showing great promise in enzymatic mimics. Furthermore, compared with metal oxide-based nanomaterials and noble metal-based nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimics are able to maintain high catalytic activity over a wide pH range [19].

Single-atomic layered graphene oxide, with its high electron conjugate state and abundant surface oxygen-containing groups, has showed peroxidase-like activity to catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) with H2O2 [20]. So far, GO has been developed as a burgeoning support to disperse and stabilize metal, metal oxide, and semiconductor nanomaterials, which can dramatically enhance the properties of the nanoparticles [21–23]. For example, GO sheets supported Fe3O4 NPs have been proved to possess enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic properties compared to either component alone [24]. The hierarchical copper sulfide decorated reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (CuS-rGO) exhibits impressive peroxidase-like activity, which results from strong covalent interaction between CuS and rGO synergistically enhances the catalytic activity of CuS-rGO in comparison to its individual counterparts [25]. However, the practicability of GO-based peroxidase mimics is quite limited due to the poor dispersibility of GO, which makes it not be able stably presented in biological medias even under very lower concentration.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene fragments with lateral sizes less than 100nm, which have several unique properties over micrometer-sized graphene and GO sheets [26,27]. With a large surface area ratio and more active sites, GQDs may serve as promising candidates as peroxidase mimics, which have the advantages of excellent water-solubility, resistance against stringent conditions, low cost and tunability in catalytic activities. GQDs have been recognized as better electron transporters and acceptors, rendering them as optimal candidates for electrochemical sensing materials [28,29]. And the abundant carboxylic groups and active sites on the surface of GQDs make it promising candidates as peroxidase mimics [26]. However, GQDs have a lower affinity for organic substrates (TMB) due to the lack of π-π and hydrophobic interactions similar to GO [18].

Recently, copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) supported on graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets has been established to present high electrocatalysis and power density [30]. However, few investigations have been conducted systematically on their peroxidase-like catalysis activities especially their catalysis performances in the aqueous solution, which could be challenged by the poor aqueous dispersion of hydrophobic carbon supports and the undesirable spatial distribution of CuO nanocatalysts loaded. It is worthy of notice that the CuO NPs could serve as a metal oxide-based peroxidase mimic which could be able to maintain a higher affinity for TMB than that of HRP [31]. Thus, in this work, we develop a simple route to prepare GQDs enriched with periphery carboxylic groups at a mass scale through a microwave-hydrothermal protocol. Then the well dispersed GQDs are adopted as supports for the growth of CuO nanoneedles to produce GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. The as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites show enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic activity than individual CuO NPs, GQDs and their physical mixture, which might largely result from the strong synergistic interaction between GQDs and CuO nanoneedles. Finally, the as-prepared GQDs/CuO composites are employed for the oxidation of TMB to evolve blue coloration in the presence of H2O2. As H2O2 is a main product in the glucose oxidase (GOx) catalytic reaction, combining the GQDs/CuO composites and GOx catalytic property, a colorimetric method with favorable performances for the detection of H2O2 and glucose is thus developed, which is successfully applied to the detection of glucose contents in diluted serums. Attributed to the distinctive synergistic catalytic effect of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites in the TMB/H2O2 catalytic oxidation reaction, the proposed method offers improved detection sensitivity towards glucose when compared with other assays based on nanomaterials with peroxidase-like activity by colorimetric determination, especially the sole CuO NPs or GQDs-based protocols.

Cupric acetate, sodium hydroxide, H2O2(30%), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), high purity graphite powder, P2O5, K2S2O8, H2SO4, KMnO4, glucose oxidase (GOx), glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), lactose (Lac), maltose (Mat), and mannose(Man), horseradish peroxidase (HRP) are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glacial acetic acid and sodium nitrate are purchased from Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All solutions are prepared with ultrapure water of 18.2MΩcm. Britton-Robinson buffer (B-R buffer, pH 3.5) is prepared by a mixture of 0.04M H3BO3, 0.04M H3PO4 and 0.04M CH3COOH. Serum samples from healthy volunteers are provided by the Northeastern University Hospital.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are obtained with a transmission electron microscope (H-7650, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 120kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements are performed by using an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo Fisher, U.S.A.). XRD patterns are recorded on an X’Pert Pro MPD X-ray diffractometer (PW 3040/60, PANalytical B.V., Holland) with Cu Kα irradiation (λ=1.5406Å). Raman spectra are obtained using an XploRA Raman spectrometer (Horiba Co., France) with an excitation wavelength at 532nm. FT-IR spectra are recorded on Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, U.S.A.) from 400 to 4000cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectra are recorded with a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan).

Graphene oxide (GO) is firstly prepared from natural graphite powder according to the Hummers and Offeman method [32]. The graphite powder is treated by pre-oxidation with P2O5, K2S2O8, and concentrated sulfuric acid before use. Graphene quantum dots are then prepared by a one-step hydrothermal procedure [33]. Briefly, the previously obtained GO solution (5mL, 10mgmL−1) is first mixed with concentrated HNO3 (8mL, 65%, v/v) carefully, thereafter the mixture is sealed into a Teflon vessel and heated at 200°C for 8min under microwave irradiation (800W, 30Mpa). After cooling down to room temperature, the reaction product is neutralized by sodium carbonate. The supernatant is collected by centrifugation and filtered with a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove the large tracts of GO, and the GQDs are finally collected after dialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of CuO nanoneedles on GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The growth of CuO nanoneedles on GQDs is achieved via a facile one-step precipitation procedure. 15mL of 0.02M copper acetate is mixed with 100μL glacial acetic acid in a round-bottomed flask. Thereafter, 5.450mL GQDs (1mgmL−1) is added and the obtained solution is completely mixed under vigorous stirring for 30min, and the reaction mixture is further heated to 100°C under vigorous stirring. Then 5mL of 0.5M NaOH is rapidly added into the above boiling solution, and the solution is kept at that temperature for 5min. After cooled down to room temperature, the formed precipitate is collected via centrifugation, washed with water and absolute ethanol in turn for 5 times, and dried for further use.

H2O2 detection is performed as following: 35μL of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites solution(1.0mgmL−1), 16μL of TMB (25mM) and 125μL of H2O2 sample are added into 324μL of 40mM B-R buffer (pH 3.5) in sequence. The mixed solution is incubated in a 40°C water bath for 25min, and then the absorbance of the resultant solution at 652nm is recorded for H2O2 detection. The calibration graph is derived on the basis of the relationship between the concentration of H2O2 and the absorbance at 652nm.

Glucose detection is performed as following: 100μL of GOx aqueous solution (1.0mgmL−1) and 300μL of glucose solution with different concentrations (glucose is dissolved in 0.01M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0)) are mixed completely and incubated at 37°C for 30min; then 96μL of TMB (25mM), 210μL of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites solution (1.0mgmL−1) and 2.295mL of B-R buffer (40mM, pH 3.5) are successively added to the glucose reaction system. Finally, the mixed solution is incubated at 40°C for 25min, and the absorbance of the resultant solution at 652nm is thus recorded for glucose detection.

The morphologies of GQDs and GQDs/CuO nanocomposites are identified by TEM, as illustrated in Fig. 1 A and B. The TEM observations show that the GQDs (Fig. 1A) are uniform small round sheets with a relatively narrow size distribution between 2 and 7nm, which is similar to previous reports [34]. Fig. 1B reveals that the as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites has a spindle-shaped structure of 80±10nm in length and 16±5nm in width. The specific morphology could be ascribed to the presence of surface groups or functionalities on the GQDs surface, which may pose substantial influence on the crystal growth process of CuO NPs [35,36]. Fig. 1C shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. It suggests that the CuO are polycrystalline microstructure consisting of some small nanocrystallites with different orientations. Fig. 1D shows the FT-IR spectra of GQDs and GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. In the spectrum of GQDs, the intense band at 3432cm−1 is attributed to the stretching of OH. The band at 1725cm−1 is associated with the stretching of the CO bond. The peak at 1615cm−1 can be assigned to the skeletal vibrations of unoxidized graphene domains (aromatic CC). Deformation of the CO band is observed at 1250cm−1. After the growth of CuO nanoneedles, the peak of CO stretching vibration shifts from 1725cm−1 to 1622cm−1, indicating that the periphery carboxylic groups of the GQDs have been converted into carboxylate groups. This result well suggests that the binding of CuO nanoneedles on GQDs is achieved via CuO bonds [20]. At the same time, the growth of CuO nanoneedles on GQDs surface leads to the weakening of the vibrational absorption of CO and the disappearance of the vibration band of epoxy groups at 1250cm−1. Moreover, the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites differs from that of GQDs by the new band at 598cm−1, 529cm−1 and 433cm−1, which can be attributed to the characteristic stretching vibrations of CuO bond in monoclinic CuO. The high-frequency mode at 598cm−1 should be from the stretching vibration of CuO along the [−101] direction, and the mode ate 529cm−1 is stretching vibration of CuO along the [101] direction [37]. Moreover, no band is observed at 615cm−1, which is the infrared-active modes of Cu2O, suggesting the solely existence of CuO in the obtained nanocomposites [37–39].

Fig. 1E shows the XRD results of the as-prepared GQDs and GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. GQDs show a weak broad peak (002), which is ascribed to the thinness and disordered stacking of some GQDs [40]. The interlayer spacing is 0.363nm, which is broader than that of pristine graphite (0.334) [41]. This result could be attributed to the oxygen-containing groups introduced in the exfoliation and oxidation of GO, which enhances the interlayer distance. For GQDs/CuO nanocomposites, systematic XRD analysis suggests the presence of a monoclinic phase of CuO with the refraction peaks at 32.5° (110), 35.4° (002), 38.6° (111/200), 48.7° (−202), 53.5° (020), 61.4° (202), 65.6° (−113), 67.9° (022/−311), 74.9° (004/−222) and 82.7° (222) (ICDD-PDF Card No. 01-089-5897) in the obtained nanocomposites. No characteristic peaks of Cu(OH)2 and Cu2O is observed, indicating the high purity of CuO product in the obtained nanocomposites.

XPS analysis is carried out to investigate the oxidation level and chemical bonding nature of the obtained. There are only C1s peak (284eV) and O1s peak (532eV) involved in the full range XPS of originally prepared GQDs (Fig. 1F). In the XPS spectra of GQDs/CuO composites, apart from the C1s and O1s peaks, the peaks at 75.2 and 123.2eV are attributed to Cu3p and Cu3s, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 569.7 and 649eV can be ascribed to Cu LMM Auger peaks. It is known that the Cu (0) (metallic copper) (LMM) Auger peak has a binding energy of about 567eV, while the binding energy of oxidized Cu (I) and Cu (II) (LMM) is about 569eV [42,43]. Therefore we can infer that Cu is not present in the form of monatomic copper, but in the form of oxides in the obtained composite. The peak around 942eV clearly observed in the wide-scan XPS spectrum of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites corresponds to copper 2p3 [30], which is greater than that of standard value of metallic copper (932.7eV). This further indicates that Cu exists in the form of oxides in the composites.

The high-resolution Cu 2p spectra of the GQDs/CuO composites can be clearly deconvoluted into four components (Fig. 1G). The peaks corresponding to the core-level 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 transitions of Cu locate at 933.9eV and 953.9eV, respectively. The gap between these levels is 20eV, which agrees well with that of standard spectrum of CuO [40,44]. Moreover, the typical shake-up satellite peaks appear within the binding energy range from 940 to 945eV further confirms the existence of Cu(II), which is consistent with the reported XRD results [43,45].

Raman spectroscopy plays a critical role in characterization of graphene materials [46] and is employed here to gain information about structure and defects of the GQDs. In Fig. 1H, the peaks of D and G modes, corresponding to disordered sp3 defects and the sp2 CC bond, are observed at around 1345cm−1 and 1590cm−1 in both the spectra of GQDs and GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. However, the ID/IG ratio of GQDs/CuO composites (0.76) is lower than that of GQDs (0.87), suggesting that the reduction of defects during the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites fabrication process [47]. At the same time, the Raman peaks at 281cm−1, 360cm−1and 610cm−1 assigned to the Ag and Bg vibration modes CuO [48,49], are clearly observed in the spectrum of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites, which support the existence of CuO in the obtained nanocomposites.

Based on the above results, we proposed a possible growth mechanism for the formation of GQDs/CuO composites, as illustrate in Scheme 1 . GQDs is a kind of single atom-thick graphene sheet bearing oxygen-containing functional groups on their basal planes and edges. These functional groups can act as anchor sites and facilitates the in-situ growth of metal NPs on the surface and edge of GQDs. After the mixing of GQDs with copper acetate, the Cu2+ cation is prone to bind with the oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs via electrostatic attraction. With the addition of NaOH, Cu(OH)2 is formed gradually and deposited on the surface of the GQDs. As Cu(OH)2 is a metastable phase and prone to transform into stable CuO via thermal dehydration in aqueous phase [50], therefore, spindle-shaped CuO nanoneedles are formed on GQDs by self-assembly from the intermediate Cu(OH)2 [51].

To investigate the peroxidase-like activity of the as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites, catalytic oxidations of the chromogenic substrate TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) with GQDs/CuO nanocomposites in the presence or absence of H2O2 are tested.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites+TMB system is colorless, and the TMB+H2O2 system without GQDs/CuO nanocomposites shows negligible color variation under experimental conditions. In contrast, the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites+TMB+H2O2 system produce a dark blue solution. These results clearly suggest the obtained GQDs/CuO composites own excellent peroxidase-like activity. Our results also indicate that the peroxidase-like activity GQDs/CuO composites is higher than that of GO/CuO composites. The reason might lay in the fact that nanometersized GQDs sheets exhibit higher activity than the micrometer-sized GO sheets, as GQDs have more intact aromatic structure and are rich in periphery carboxylic groups [18,26,52]. Fig. 2B illustrates the catalytic behaviors of GQDs, CuO NPs, the mixture of GQDs and CuO NPs, and the obtained GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. Under the same conditions, the highest catalytic rate is achieved by GQDs/CuO nanocomposites, indicating that the synergistic effect of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites via the growth of CuO NPs on GQDs, rather than the simple physical combination of two components.

To further understand the kinetic mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites, apparent steady-state kinetic parameters of the catalytic oxidations reaction are determined by changing the concentrations of TMB and H2O2, respectively. The kinetic data are obtained by varying one substrate concentration while keeping the other substrate concentration constant. A series of initial reaction rates are calculated and applied to the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation,
(1)
1
v
=
(
K
m
V max
)
⋅
(
1
[ S ]
)
+
1
V max

where ν is the initial velocity, [S] is the concentration of the substrate, K m is the Michaelies-Menten constant and V max is the maximal reaction velocity. It is observed that the oxidation reaction catalyzed by the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites follow the typical Michaelis-Menten behavior towards both substrates TMB and H2O2 (Fig. 3 A and B).

The results obtained from Lineweaver-Burk plots indicate that the K m value of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites with H2O2 as the substrate is much lower than that of CuO NPs and N-GQDs [19], as summarized in Table 1 . This suggests that lower H2O2 concentration is needed for GQDs/CuO nanocomposites to achieve maximum reaction velocity than that for N-GQDs and CuO NPs [2]. Actually, K m has been identified as an indicator of enzyme affinity to substrates. The smaller the value of K m, the stronger is the affinity between the enzyme and the substrate [53]. The low K m value suggests that the growth of CuO NPs on GQDs improves the catalytic affinity toward H2O2 notably, which offers the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites higher affinity to H2O2 than individual CuO NPs and GQDs.

Fig. 3C and D illustrates the catalysis activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites by varying TMB concentration at a fixed concentration of H2O2 or vice versa. The double-reciprocal plots present the characteristic parallel lines of a ping-pong mechanism, which is similar to that observed for HRP [18,56]. These results indicate that GQDs/CuO nanocomposites bind and react with the first substrate during the catalysis procedure, then release the first product before reacting with the second substrate, which is similar to HRP and some other nanomaterials [18,57].

In order to achieve the best catalytic performance for GQDs/CuO nanocomposites, the effect of some important parameters for nanocomposites preparation including the concentration of (CH3COO)2Cu, GQDs and NaOH, the volume of glacial acetic acid solution, reaction temperature and time are investigated (Fig.S1). A GQDs concentration of 1mgmL−1, a (CH3COO)2Cu concentration of 0.02M, a NaOH concentration of 0.5M, a CH3COOH volume of 100μL, a reaction temperature of 100°C and a reaction time of 30min are finally adopted for the preparation of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites

The stability of the as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites is tested by incubating GQDs/CuO nanocomposites at different temperature and pH range for 2h, and then their own activities are measured under standard conditions. The GQDs/CuO nanocomposites are indeed found to remain stable over a wide range of temperature from 20°C to 60°C. In contrast, the catalytic activity of HRP dramatically declined after treatment at temperatures greater than 30°C (Fig.S2A). At the same time, GQDs/CuOnanocomposites in weak acidic/alkali condition exhibit much better stability than HRP (Fig.S2 B). Therefore the robustness of GQDs/CuOnanocomposites makes them potentially applicable under harsh conditions.

Scheme 2 illustrates the possible catalytic process of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites. Firstly, H2O2 molecules are adsorbed on the surface of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites and then activated by the Cu2+ center to generate high reactive hydroxyl species (OH) [25,58]. Thereafter, this newly generated OH actually oxidizes TMB into its blue colored form.

In order to demonstrate that the mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites is originated from the generation of high reactive OH, terephthalic acid is added into the reaction system, as terephthalic acid can be converted into fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid by OH [55]. The results indicate that there is no fluorescence intensity in the absence of H2O2, while the fluorescence of the system in the presence of H2O2 increases with the concentration of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites (Fig. 4 ), suggesting the increasing amount of OH radical groups produced by the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of GQDs/CuO composites in H2O2 detection and glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to NPs-based peroxidase mimetics and HRP, the catalytic activity of GQDs/CuO composites is dependent on temperature, pH and TMB concentration. Fig. 5 illustrates the peroxidase-like activities of GQDs/CuO composites under different temperature (Fig. 5A), system pH (Fig. 5B) and TMB concentration (Fig. 5C). It can be seen that the best catalytic activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites towards H2O2 is achieved under the temperature of 40°C, system pH of 3.5 and TMB of 0.8mM, therefore, the parameters are adopted for ensuing H2O2 detection.

As shown in Fig. 6 A, the absorbance of the oxidized TMB product at 652nm displays a positive curvature to the concentration of H2O2 within the range of 0–200μM, and it fits a linear regression equation of A=0.01674CH2O2 +0.02420 over the range of 0.5–10μM. The limit of detection for H2O2 is derived to be 0.17μM (3σ/K), which is much lower than the detection limit of individual CuO NPs and GQDs [19,31].

As H2O2 is the main product of glucose oxidase (GOx)-catalyzed reaction, the colorimetric detection of glucose also could be realized by using GQDs/CuO composites as the artificial enzyme. As illustrated in Fig. 6B, the absorbance at 652nm is proportional to the glucose concentration in the range 2–100μM with a detection limit of 0.59μM (3σ/K). The linear regression equation is A=0.00255Cglucose +0.07363 with a correlation coefficient of 0.994. The color variation induced by glucose is also obvious by visual observation, even under a glucose concentration lower to 5μM (Fig. 6B inset). Table 2 summarizes the analytical performance of the present glucose detection system with some reported procedures. The GQDs/CuO-based detection system offers better performance than the reported electrochemical and fluorimetric approaches, i.e., the sensitivity of GQDs/CuO-based colorimetric detection system is higher that CuO NPs- or GQDs-based fluorimetric methods. More significantly, the GQDs/CuO nanocomposites are superior to other nanomaterials with peroxidase-like activity by colorimetric determination, the detection limit is improved by 1–2 order of magnitudes when compared with CuO NPs or GQDs-based protocols.

The selectivity for glucose detection is evaluated under the co-existence of mannose, lactose, fructose and maltose. As shown in Fig. 7 , the absorbance for glucose is much higher than that of co-existent components, even when the concentration of co-existent components is 10 times higher than that of glucose. These results suggest the favorable selectivity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites in the detection of glucose.

The proposed assay is thus applied to the detection of glucose in serum samples. Serum samples are firstly centrifuged at 3000rpm for 15min, and the supernatant is collected and diluted with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.0) for 100 times. Thereafter, 300μL of the resulting diluted filtrate is taken for the glucose detection as described in the experimental section, and the results are summarized in Table 3 . Given the fact that the glucose concentrations in serum samples are in the general range of diabetic (9–40mM) and healthy persons (3–8mM) [65], the results obtained using present method are close to those provided by the hospital. Compared with the instrumental methods for glucose determination, in which natural enzymes such as glucose oxidase and dehydrogenase are usually needed, the proposed assay based on GQDs/CuO nanocomposites are merited with simplicity and economy, indicating the practicability of this colorimetric method for glucose sensing in serum samples.

In present study, we succeed in preparing GQDs/CuO nanocomposites by the in-situ growth of CuO nanoneedles on the surface of GQDs, without the use of any templates or surfactants. The as-prepared GQDs/CuO nanocomposites show improved peroxidase-like catalytic activity than CuO and GQDs, which may be contributed by the maximum synergistic interactions between GQDs and CuO nanoneedles. The colorimetric methods base on the catalytic activity of GQDs/CuO nanocomposites for H2O2 and glucose detection not only exhibit higher sensitivity than other NPs-based detection protocols, but also show better stability than horseradish peroxidase, offering the obtained GQDs/CuO nanocomposites great potential in medical diagnostics and environmental chemistry.

Though more efforts will be necessarily made to better understand the catalysis mechanism underlying for the carbon-supported CuO nanocatalysts, the proposed preparation method for inorganic nanocatalysts with well-dispersed carbon supports might open a new way for the design of powerful “artificial enzymes”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475017, 21235010 and 21275027), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N150502001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.04.011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Zhang received bachelor degree in Chemistry from Huanghuai College University (China) in 2014 and Master (2016) in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University (China). She is currently a Ph.D candidate in the Department of Chemistry at Northeastern University. Her main research interest is in nano-material-based sensors.

Xin Hai received her Master degree (2015) in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University (China). She now is a Ph.D student at the Research Centre for Analytical Sciences in Northeastern University, China. Her work is devoted to the application of graphene quantum dots in protein assays.

Chang Xia received bachelor degree (2014) and Master degree (2016) in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University (China). She is currently a Ph.D candidate in the Department of Chemistry at Northeastern University. Her work is focus on the applications of graphene quantum dots.

Xu-wei Chen is a professor at the Research Centre for Analytical Sciences in Northeastern University, China. Prof. Chen received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University(China) in July 2007. His current research interests focus on the development of new techniques and materials for the isolation of trace biomacromolecules from complex biological samples.

Jian-hua Wang is currently a professor at the Research Centre for Analytical Sciences in Northeastern University, China. He obtained his B.Sc from the Department of Chemistry, Nankai University, and M.Sc from Jilin University, China. He was awarded a PhD degree by Technical University of Denmark in 2002. He has been serving as an Associate Editor for TALANTA since Dec. 2004. His current research interest is focused on the exploitation of flow analysis protocols and hyphenation techniques, isolation of trace level of analytes of biologically significant (metals and biomacromolecules) from complex sample matrices and life science analysis.
